item.Artistlist: C, Charles Tiffin (stone mason John Petrie)
item.Description: Walter Hill Fountain commemorates the first curator of the Botanic Gardens, Walter Hill.

An ornate Victorian Revival Gothic style monument executed in sandstone. A memorial to James Thomas Mooney, a member of the Volunteer Fire Brigade, who died in 1877 from injuries received fighting a fire in Queen Street.
Bronze memorial of Thomas Joseph Byrnes, premier of Queensland in 1898, though died shortly after in the same year.

Bronze of Robbie Burns, a Scottish poet. Bronze plaques of scenes from his poems decorate each side of a carved sandstone plinth. The thistle cornice is dedicated to the Scottish women of Brisbane who raised funds for the memorial.
A carved sandstone tympanum in the pediment above the main entrance of City Hall. Completed after the opening of the building in 1930.

item.Itemtitlelist: K, King George V Memorial - Lions
item.Artist: E.F. Kohler, R. Summerhayes, E.S. Taylor
item.Artistlist: E, E.F. Kohler, R. Summerhayes, E.S. Taylor
item.Description: The bronze Lions were commissioned as part of the King George V memorial, which traditionally symbolise the British Empire.

item.Artist: E.F. Kohler, R. Summerhayes, E.S. Taylor
item.Artistlist: E, E.F. Kohler, R. Summerhayes, E.S. Taylor
item.Description: The King George V memorial statue was unveiled in Albert Square on 24 May 1938 following the King's death in 1936.

The Sir William Glasgow Memorial was designed by Queensland sculptor Daphne Mayo and completed in 1964. It is in the form of a naturalistic bronze figure of Sir William, dressed in Australian Light Horse uniform and set on a granite plinth.
A spiralling metal sculpture creating a central feature for the McCormick Place Fountain. The fountain is in the form of a helix water sculpture, which rises from the base of a blue-tiled pool.

Robert Juniper is considered one of Australia's most prominent interdisciplinary artists of the 20th century. 'Plant Form' was loaned by the artist for World Expo 88 and later purchased by Brisbane City Council.
Sandstone sculpture created as part of the Warana festivities and donated to the city by Besser (Qld) Ltd.

Inukshuk means, "something which acts for or performs the function of a human" - a marker or way finder. Gift from the Northwest Territories of Canada to the people of Queensland at the conclusion of World Expo '88.
Gestation is the first work in a series of monumental sculptures created by American artist Baile Oakes. Commissioned for World Expo 88.
Forme del Mito is derived from the tragic story of Agamemnon from Ancient Greece. Created by renowned Italian sculptor Arnaldo Pomodoro and exhibited at World Expo '88.

Commissioned for World Expo '88, installed near the Australian pavilion. Killick uses international media and events to create a collection of humorous motifs which is a recording of the political issues of the time.
Commissioned for World Expo '88, installed at the centre of 'Times Square' site.

Showdown was commissioned by Tasmanian sculptor Chris Beecroft for World Expo '88. Beecroft uses colour, shape and form in playful collaboration to celebrate themes of leisure in the age of technology.

The Man & Matter Series consists of twelve steel sculptures installed along the Kangaroo Point Cliffs Boardwalk. Commissioned for the riverside promenade of Brisbane's World Expo '88, referencing the Expo '88 theme of Leisure in the Age of Technology
Memories of Wind was one of four sculptures exhibited at World Expo '88 by Japanese artist Fumio Nishimura.

Juxtaposition I is a cement painted steel abstract sculpture, commissioned by Brisbane City Council in 1990 with the assistance of the Visual Arts and Crafts Board of the Australian Council of the Arts.
This work was commissioned during the bicentennial year to honour early Brisbane settlers. It specifically depicts the Petrie family and their neighbours in 1842.
The white painted steel sculpture features a bucket of flowers on a table and two framed landscapes; one real and one imaginary.
Commissioned in 1988, the sculpture formed part of the Artist in the Park programme, a Community Arts project initiated by Brisbane City Council.
Sundial with carved sandstone, installed in the City Botanic Gardens in 1993.

item.Itemtitlelist: J, Jemmy Morrill and the Brolgas
item.Description: Bronze sculptures depicting Jemmy Morrill and two brolgas with wings outstretched. 22 year old seaman, James Morrill, was the sole survivor of a shipwreck on the outer edge of the Great Barrier Reef in 1846.
This work depicts three important figures in Queensland's history; Emma Miller, Sir Charles Lilley and Steele Rudd.
Commissioned in 1993, five crooked bronze tapering points with a brushed surface.
A stylised metal fish fossil made from found materials.
Carved in Queen Street Mall during Art & Fashion week, 1995. A montage of Images that typify Brisbane.
Two recycled metal bio-mechanical Pelicans bolt fixed onto a derelict concrete and iron river pylon near the Captain Cook Bridge.

item.Location: cnr Thornton Street and Pixley Street, Kangaroo Point
item.Description: These sculptures stand as guardians or beacons of both the river and the ferry, and reference the Story Bridge and boats.
A series of six bronze sculpture depicting Australian animals attached to street lighting.
Metal flower on stem with solar powered panels on its petals that make it spin.
Full size seat for two with a cast platypus located onto the central arm rest.
Representing the natural elements of Earth, Fire & Wind this sculpture was carved in the Queen Street Mall during Art & Fashion Week.
Figures forming a ballustrade screen.

Commissioned as part of the Childrens Art Trail. Children and parents can explore the Hide 'n' Seek Children's Trail set in the heart of the shady Exotic Rainforest in the Brisbane Botanic Gardens, Mt Coot-tha.

item.Location: Railway Parade and Newman Road, Geebung
item.Description: Bronze circular plaques depicting 16 different native animals and plants used by the Turrubul people for food and medicine.
Two metal sculptures that refence the major retail trade of Stones Corner.

item.Location: Admiralty Towers pocket park, Queen Street
item.Description: Series of three Helidon sandstone sculptures depicting different types of sailors knots.
Commemorates beacons lit around Australia in the bicentenary year, 1988.

item.Location: Brighton Road, cnr Seymour Street, Sandgate
item.Description: Series of five bronze plaques celebrating local Indigenous culture.
A bronze sculpture depicting local wildlife and celebrating connections to the natural environment.
Cast bronze birds and branches. This work references the grape industry which the area was once known for.
Bronze sculpture depicting a local canine identity fixed to a sandstone plinth.
The mechanical chooks created from found industrial and farming materials provides a unique cultural link between the industrial history of Geebung and the rejuvenated shopping heart.

The 18 cast aluminium totems symbolise aspects of Aboriginal culture in Boondall Wetlands and occur along Billai Dhagun walking track, the Bikeway and at Nudgee Beach. The totems refer to food, plants and animals, tools, camp life and the Dreaming, and each totem is placed near a related site, plant or animal habitat.
Indigenous landmark piece celebrating local flora and fauna. The Goanna provides a place to rest and meet.

Memorial in remembrance of major sporting event in 1931. Aboriginal Eddie Gilbert's lightning bowling gets Don Bradman out for a duck in first Queensland v New South Wales Sheffield Shield match at Gabba.
Painted telegraph poles with carved wooden attachments, entrance to Woolcock Park, Red Hill.

A traditional Aboriginal bark canoe with crab and dilly bag cast in bronze. The traditional bark canoe was commissioned from the Boogadin Bunkoo Canoe Builders as part of the first Oxley Creek Water Festival in 1997. It acknowledges the Jagara People's use of Oxley Creek.
Celebrates local identity Gordon Fredericks.

item.Location: Charlotte and George Streets, Brisbane CBD
item.Description: References Brisbane history and replicates the front page of the original Moreton Bay Courier newspaper. Sited next to the original location of the newspaper building.
The kangaroo sculptures have been deliberately placed in a highly used pedestrian area, to demonstrate how humans need to share outdoor spaces with native animals.

item.Location: Charlotte and Albert Street, Brisbane CBD
item.Description: An abstracted human form, constructed of 598 panels and standing 3.4m tall.

item.Artistlist: H, Hew Chee Fong, L.M. Noonan
item.Description: A site generated land art installation. It operates on several levels so that it is both simple to read and understand, yet mysterious and multilayered in cryptic meaning and metaphor.
Reclining bronze Kangaroo located in the City Botanic Gardens.
Reflects the area's local fauna and symbolises the importance of harmony with nature.

This sculptural artpoem evokes the power of the Brisbane River sweeping around this curve at New Farm, triggering memories of the massive floods of 1893 and 1974, and 2011 and anxieties about the next.
Installed in 2000, Whale Tails is three timber poles topped with copper caps and steel tails.
This artwork was designed to frame the view to Mt Coot-tha, which is identifiable by the television towers that occupy its peak.

Sheridan Kennedys 'Optogemel' is a huge bejewelled disc which spins when a passerby turns a handle below. Commissioned as part of a 2001 project to produce artworks for the Brisbane powerhouse Cultural Precinct.
Cast bronze Sundial with two interlocking curved stainless steel bands, installed in Centenary Place.

This work celebrates the river's maritime history. These sculptures symbolise the last remnant of AMSAR, which was located not far from the site. AMSAR operated as a maintenance workshop for marine navigational lights.
Horizontal and vertical spirals reflect the concept of growth and the energy of living things, and the elements of life, wind and fire.
A series of lightboxes set into pillars at the entrances of Merthyr Park. The lightboxes contain images and collected materials related to the local foods gathered in Brisbane.

item.Artistlist: H, Hew Chee Fong, L.M. Noonan
item.Description: The sculptures represent the spiral forms found in most shells of both marine and land creatures and playfully suggest that they have in eons past been caught and lodged in this catchment area.

item.Location: Tavistock Street, Cook Street, Oxley Station Road & Lincoln Street, Oxley
item.Description: These five individual pieces of artwork are representative of the Oxley Creek aquatic life. Their connection with the granite boulders symbolises their connection with the area.
Two cast bronze cormorant figures installed as standing birds in the lagoon.
This sculpture is based on the environmentally sensitive Mayfly. The adult Mayfly is a small, short-lived insect that is a valuable indicator of water quality.
Celebrates the pioneering spirit, domestic work, and strength of women past and future.
A series of signs with humorous messages from literature, public spaces and culture.

item.Itemtitlelist: B, Bulka Booangun - Calling the Dolphins
item.Description: Part of the Quandamooka Jetty Art project by the Rotary Club of Wynnum and Manly and the Winnam Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islanders Corporation. It Commemorates the original timber jetty built in 1882.

item.Location: Rocks Riverside Park, Seventeen Mile Rocks
item.Description: Morning Glory serves as a mythical guardian for Rocks Riverside Park. The significance of the Brisbane River; the leftover landscape; prehistoric inhabitation; the freakiness yet reality of the gill and lung breathing creature - the Qld Lungfish (Ceratodus), and the natural spiralling beauty and symmetry found in coral and shells, were inspiration for this work.

item.Location: Rocks Riverside Park, Seventeen Mile Rocks
item.Description: From the process of harvesting Moreton Bay coral for lime for concrete, our co-dependency with our environment is once again up for review.

item.Location: Cnr Cairns Street, Deakin Street and Bradfield Hwy, Kangaroo Point
item.Description: Made of recycled highway barriers, commissioned by Story Bridge Precinct Alliance and the Precinct's gateway element.

The steel arc connects to and resonates with the river, the tides and the flow of time. The colour of the arc 'ultramarine blue' represents the ocean depths. The rock elements are layered like a contour of a map and have been based on echo sounding charts of the riverbed.

item.Location: Mollison, Boundary and Melbourne Street, South Brisbane
item.Description: Cast integrated units (pods & tendrils) inserted into planted bed walls.

item.Location: Cnr Edmonstone and Melbourne Street, South Brisbane
item.Description: 6 Latin phrases with botanical and local themes set flush into planter bed walls.

item.Artist: Luke Roberts, Urban Arts Projects - UAP
item.Artistlist: L, Luke Roberts, Urban Arts Projects - UAP
item.Description: Unclassified - invokes ideas of the unknown, unfamiliar and the unchartered. Flowering - a semi-abstract, biomorphic shape based on a floral emblem Flower of Life Organism - a living form, depicting movement and vitality
References the tram tracks leading to the original terminus.
Site specific memorial to Cloudland Ballroom. The design is based on the famous Cloudland arch and entrance way.

item.Location: Mollison, Boundary and Melbourne Street Boulevard, South Brisbane
item.Description: 20 suspended flowering/organic shapes made from aluminium plate.

Echoes from the North was installed in 2005 and is a connector between Brisbane City and Northern Queensland. The lush green and iridescent white of the central form are reminders of the rainforest and tropical plant life. The metallic reds, blues and yellows are a reflection of the iridescent tropical hues of the fish found on the Great Barrier Reef with the blue of the sea and the golden yellow to the sun a reminder of the beach and maritime activities.

Commissioned as part of the Childrens Art Trail. Children and parents can explore the Hide 'n' Seek Children's Trail set in the heart of the shady Exotic Rainforest in the Brisbane Botanic Gardens, Mt Coot-tha.

A bronze figure was created for members of the SOICA (Survivors of Institutional Childhood Abuse). The artwork is in memory of all the children who suffered abuse and those who did not survive in Queensland.

item.Itemtitlelist: P, Place of the land tortoise
item.Description: Created in situ during the 2006 Creation Symposium in New Farm Park. Holden ute carved from a single block of white marble, with number plate RIP-CAR. Black marble details include ute tray cover, tear under headlight and four components on the rear including two brake lights.

Commissioned as part of the Childrens Art Trail. Children and parents can explore the Hide 'n' Seek Children's Trail set in the heart of the shady Exotic Rainforest in the Brisbane Botanic Gardens, Mt Coot-tha.
This work is both a sculptural and sound based work, its form is a large shell carved from marble.

item.Location: Merthyr Park, Sydney Street, New Farm
item.Description: This work is representative of Australian and Japanese artists working together. The ships depict the Japanese artist coming to Australia. The work is carved Keperra Granite.

item.Location: Corner Elizabeth Street and Beaudesert Road, Acacia Ridge
item.Description: Acacia Ridge Fossil was created as a significant gateway artwork that will attract passing motorists to the centre. It is comprised of locally sourced materials and discarded objects from the motor, rail and farming industries, some dating back to the 1800's.
Provides a large-scale entry marker as part of the Alderley roundabout upgrade.

Commissioned as part of the Childrens Art Trail. Children and parents can explore the Hide 'n' Seek Children's Trail set in the heart of the shady Exotic Rainforest in the Brisbane Botanic Gardens, Mt Coot-tha.

In 1998, 5 ceremonial plaques were placed around significant sites in Brisbane near institutions that housed victims of the stolen generation. In the years 1900-1971, as a matter of government policy Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander children were forcibly removed from their families and culture by representatives of the State with the support of the wider community including Christian churches.
Surge is a response to the running wave phenomenon experienced on king and high tides, which travels the length of Lovers Walk collapsing back into the sea near the artwork.
A pole mounted marker composed of found materials which functions as an entrance to the industrial area.
Entrance to the Childrens Art Trail. Children and parents can explore the Hide 'n' Seek Children's Trail set in the heart of the shady Exotic Rainforest in the Brisbane Botanic Gardens, Mt Coot-tha.
Zoom! Zap! and Blat! reference the energy forces within the flow of movement through the Cycle Centre and the dynamics of this energised transitional place.
Billow is a site-specific work inspired by the full spinnakers of the sailing boats, as they come close to the piers end for their final jibe into the home straight.

Konstantin Tsiolkosky was a Russian provincial teacher and a scientist far ahead of his time. His thoughts were of the Universe and human flight into space. Tsiolkosky was born in Russia, but his scientific discoveries belong to the world.

Commissioned as part of the Childrens Art Trail. Children and parents can explore the Hide 'n' Seek Children's Trail set in the heart of the shady Exotic Rainforest in the Brisbane Botanic Gardens, Mt Coot-tha.

item.Artist: Lee-Anne Elms, Margot MacManus, Rosalynde Skewes
item.Artistlist: L, Lee-Anne Elms, Margot MacManus, Rosalynde Skewes
item.Description: Commissioned as part of the Childrens Art Trail. Children and parents can explore the Hide 'n' Seek Children's Trail set in the heart of the shady Exotic Rainforest in the Brisbane Botanic Gardens, Mt Coot-tha.

The Guardian is a contemporary mythological figure - half dog, half man. The sphere in the centre of his body represents his life force, and the tree in his backpack represents the fragility of nature and our link to it.

item.Itemtitlelist: G, Gargantuans of the Forest Floor
item.Description: Commissioned as part of the Childrens Art Trail. Children and parents can explore the Hide 'n' Seek Children's Trail set in the heart of the shady Exotic Rainforest in the Brisbane Botanic Gardens, Mt Coot-tha.

Primordial Trees delves into different territory by going back to the past, but presents 'nature' in a way that is evocative to the pedestrian user of the park and at the same time establishes an altered perception of the site that transcends this particular point in time.
Two curved rows of square metal poles installed in the garden bed of the Kemp Place park. The poles are irregularly spaced and consist of 8 poles in the back row and 12 poles in the front row.

Commissioned as part of the Childrens Art Trail. Children and parents can explore the Hide 'n' Seek Children's Trail set in the heart of the shady Exotic Rainforest in the Brisbane Botanic Gardens, Mt Coot-tha.

Commissioned as part of the Childrens Art Trail. Children and parents can explore the Hide 'n' Seek Children's Trail set in the heart of the shady Exotic Rainforest in the Brisbane Botanic Gardens, Mt Coot-tha.
The collection of elements, themed on native flora and fauna, are intended to be read as both art pieces that appeal to the adult eye, and play items attractive to children.

Commissioned as part of the Childrens Art Trail. Children and parents can explore the Hide 'n' Seek Children's Trail set in the heart of the shady Exotic Rainforest in the Brisbane Botanic Gardens, Mt Coot-tha.

Commissioned as part of the Childrens Art Trail. Children and parents can explore the Hide 'n' Seek Children's Trail set in the heart of the shady Exotic Rainforest in the Brisbane Botanic Gardens, Mt Coot-tha.

Commissioned as part of the Childrens Art Trail. Children and parents can explore the Hide 'n' Seek Children's Trail set in the heart of the shady Exotic Rainforest in the Brisbane Botanic Gardens, Mt Coot-tha.

Commissioned as part of the Childrens Art Trail. Children and parents can explore the Hide 'n' Seek Children's Trail set in the heart of the shady Exotic Rainforest in the Brisbane Botanic Gardens, Mt Coot-tha.

Commissioned as part of the Childrens Art Trail. Children and parents can explore the Hide 'n' Seek Children's Trail set in the heart of the shady Exotic Rainforest in the Brisbane Botanic Gardens, Mt Coot-tha.

Commissioned as part of the Childrens Art Trail. Children and parents can explore the Hide 'n' Seek Children's Trail set in the heart of the shady Exotic Rainforest in the Brisbane Botanic Gardens, Mt Coot-tha.

Commissioned as part of the Childrens Art Trail. Children and parents can explore the Hide 'n' Seek Children's Trail set in the heart of the shady Exotic Rainforest in the Brisbane Botanic Gardens, Mt Coot-tha.
Epificus is a chain-like structure formed from a simple pattern of repeating modules, creating a link of strength and unity.

Commissioned as part of the Childrens Art Trail. Children and parents can explore the Hide 'n' Seek Children's Trail set in the heart of the shady Exotic Rainforest in the Brisbane Botanic Gardens, Mt Coot-tha.

Commissioned as part of the Childrens Art Trail. Children and parents can explore the Hide 'n' Seek Children's Trail set in the heart of the shady Exotic Rainforest in the Brisbane Botanic Gardens, Mt Coot-tha.

item.Location: Kemp Place, McLachlan Street, Fortitude Valley
item.Description: Intentionally ambiguous, the sign is a continuation of Moody's large public text works which create a place for philosophical contemplation in the urban environment. The work is purposefully designed to look like graffiti in it's dull colour and bare concrete background.

Commissioned as part of the Childrens Art Trail. Children and parents can explore the Hide 'n' Seek Children's Trail set in the heart of the shady Exotic Rainforest in the Brisbane Botanic Gardens, Mt Coot-tha.

Commissioned as part of the Childrens Art Trail. Children and parents can explore the Hide 'n' Seek Children's Trail set in the heart of the shady Exotic Rainforest in the Brisbane Botanic Gardens, Mt Coot-tha.

Commissioned as part of the Childrens Art Trail. Children and parents can explore the Hide 'n' Seek Children's Trail set in the heart of the shady Exotic Rainforest in the Brisbane Botanic Gardens, Mt Coot-tha.

Commissioned as part of the Childrens Art Trail. Children and parents can explore the Hide 'n' Seek Children's Trail set in the heart of the shady Exotic Rainforest in the Brisbane Botanic Gardens, Mt Coot-tha.

item.Itemtitle: The King and the Creek Cray
item.Itemtitlelist: T, The King and the Creek Cray
item.Description: Commissioned as part of the Childrens Art Trail. Children and parents can explore the Hide 'n' Seek Children's Trail set in the heart of the shady Exotic Rainforest in the Brisbane Botanic Gardens, Mt Coot-tha.

item.Artistlist: H, Hew Chee Fong, L.M. Noonan
item.Description: The Open Door installation symbolizes the close knit, welcoming community of Banyo; past and present.

item.Location: Hale Street & Coronation Drive, Brisbane CBD
item.Description: The work presents a blue three dimensional illusion of movement reflecting the river experience for commuters when travelling through the bikeway culvert, offering a unique and dynamic perspective.

Amphibian Heart is a mantle enclosing a system that pumps and filters river water for the Perched Lake. Mills use of transparent and reflective materials, such as polished stainless steel, glass and water, allow her artworks to hover on the border of the physical while the geometry anchors the forms to our time and place.

Subtropical Treasure depicts rainforest plants that once grew in the area including tree ferns, palms, stinging plants, orchids, wild ginger, wild berries and fungi. Leaves at the base represent the forest floor.

item.Location: Corner Little Roma and Lower Turbot, Brisbane CBD
item.Description: The suspended sculpture is about a freshwater lens, which is a body of fresh water that pools beneath coral atolls and sand islands, such as Queensland's Heron Island and Stradbroke Island. The fresh water floats atop the salty seawater, its weight pushing out into a lens shape. Watson describes these as hidden, fragile, precious jewels, sustaining the environment and all life forms.

Commissioned as part of the Childrens Art Trail. Children and parents can explore the Hide 'n' Seek Children's Trail set in the heart of the shady Exotic Rainforest in the Brisbane Botanic Gardens, Mt Coot-tha.

item.Location: Merivale Street Rail Bridge Riverside Drive Kurilpa Point
item.Description: Cell acknowledges individuals who were placed in correctional facilities in Queensland as young people.

item.Location: cnr Wynnum Road and Riding Road, Bulimba
item.Description: The artist has drawn inspiration from themes that resonate with the local community including Bulimba's industrial history of shipping, ferries and tin smelters.

item.Itemtitle: Venus Rising: Out of the Water into the Light
item.Itemtitlelist: V, Venus Rising: Out of the Water into the Light
item.Description: The sculpture is a contemporary marker for the south eastern end of Kangaroo Point Park, acting as a portal between river and sky.

Designed to capture the eye of passing drivers and local shoppers, the artwork depicts a young boy looking through his binoculars at the city whilst his younger sister pleads for a chance to have a look.
Sea Pitcher is evocative of finger-like mangrove seed pods that hang beneath the mangrove branches.

Commissioned as part of the Childrens Art Trail. Children and parents can explore the Hide 'n' Seek Children's Trail set in the heart of the shady Exotic Rainforest in the Brisbane Botanic Gardens, Mt Coot-tha.

item.Itemtitle: Stones Corner: Any person who has organised their life, their work and themself if a genuine artist.
item.Itemtitlelist: S, Stones Corner: Any person who has organised their life, their work and themself if a genuine artist.
item.Location: Cnr Logan Road and Old Cleveland Road, Stones Corner
item.Description: Sculptures by highly renowned Scott Redford feature bright colours, text and lighting and bring vibrancy to the Stone's Corner precinct.

Reinterpreting imagery by notable Brisbane identities, CT White, Silvester Diggles and Oscar Tiegs, Preservation draws connections between the past and present and the relationship between people, flora, fauna and place, reminding us to reflect upon the diversity and vitality of the site.

item.Itemtitle: Stones Corner: Any person who has organised their life, their work and themself if a genuine artist.
item.Itemtitlelist: S, Stones Corner: Any person who has organised their life, their work and themself if a genuine artist.
item.Location: Cnr Logan Road and Old Cleveland Road, Stones Corner
item.Description: Sculptures by highly renowned Scott Redford feature bright colours, text and lighting and bring vibrancy to the Stone's Corner precinct.

Echo Orbit the cables and panels on each of the paired shade structures are installed in a parabolic grid, creating an elliptical opening in each one. Each opening is like a lens which brings into focus the infinitely rhythmic and orbital space above our heads.
A series of figures installed throughout Frew Park in Milton, which refers to a history of play and recreational memories within the Parklands site history.

item.Itemtitle: Apparatus for Germination of Achaemienis Ambulatii (Pony Plant)
item.Itemtitlelist: A, Apparatus for Germination of Achaemienis Ambulatii (Pony Plant)
item.Description: Stainless steel, bronze, copper, custom water dripping system creating a children's play area.

A series of seven small sculptures located along Hawkins Drive. The artwork attempts to draw together this socially diverse community and create a sense of friendship, welcome, cheerfulness, warmth and harmony. It will celebrate the importance of the natural environment to this community and the growth and aspirations of its members.
Located near Echo Orbit, this integrated artwork provides a central feature within the Mall. Yellow concrete ellipses with sandblasted text.

item.Location: Turbot, Wickham and Boundary streets, Fortitude Valley
item.Description: Spinning Top is a standalone sculpture of a replica of the dome of the Holy Name Cathedral which was never built.

item.Location: Cnr Barrack and Wynnum Road, Cannon Hill
item.Description: Tree celebrates the natural and cultural history of Cannon Hill, referencing an account of how the suburb was named. The story in question suggests that early surveyors of the area saw fallen trees and thought they looked like cannons.
The rhythms and patterns of nature can be reflected in the growth and aspiration of community. Growth explores the history and stories which have shaped the identity and community of Kenmore.

item.Location: Cnr Bay Terrace and Florence Street, Wynnum
item.Description: These tools of enjoyment and fun have held an important place in the historical development of Wynnum and automatically ignite a celebration of its coastal location.

item.Material: epoxy resin, slate powder, fibreglass and steel
item.Description: The forms of Reverie I has been derived from 18th century sculptural portraiture. The twisting forms of the highly styled wig known as a periwig were abstracted and inventive, while also bestowing an air of intellectual authority. Curls also evoke two aspects of this particular site: the erratic movement of water associated with the complex tidal movements of Brisbane River, and a state of mental reflection relevant to both the nearby university grounds (where intellectual work takes place) and the riverside pathway (a site for daydreaming).

These Graceville Wunderkammer or cabinet of curiosities will contain objects of local social history, natural history and will be housed in a quirky, offbeat replica of an antique metal dentist cabinet.

The sculpture references Brisbane's official floral emblem, the Poinsettia, and celebrates the annual Riverfire Festival and other popular fireworks displays which take place throughout the year in Brisbane.

item.Location: Rocks Riverside Park, Seventeen Mile Rocks
item.Description: Lighter Than Air Concrete plays on the former occupation of the site by Queensland Cement & Lime.

item.Itemtitle: Suburban Wilderness Trail - Beasties of Alderley
item.Itemtitlelist: S, Suburban Wilderness Trail - Beasties of Alderley
item.Artist: Mark Gerada, Tega Brain, Diego Bonetto
item.Artistlist: M, Mark Gerada, Tega Brain, Diego Bonetto
item.Description: Series of 15 metal signs featuring stories, drawings and information of various species in habiting the neighbourhood.

Lounge Around creates an illusion of relaxation with mosaic pattern acting as a throw rug atop a wide ottoman. The scatter cushion seems soft with its indentations and folds in the fabric, resting next to a stack of books. The cover of the book at the top of the stack is handcut mosaic that reads 'Turrbal Country' as its title, in respect of the Indigenous people of the Brisbane region prior to European settlement.

item.Location: Samford Rd and South Pine Rd, Alderley
item.Description: Terry Summers' series of 12 cast aluminium sculptures celebrate the social character and values of Alderley, as well as portraying the natural and cultural context of the suburb's past and present.

item.Itemtitle: Apparatus for Transtemporal Praedicto of Prodigium Futurum
item.Itemtitlelist: A, Apparatus for Transtemporal Praedicto of Prodigium Futurum
item.Location: Cnr Honour Ave and Bank Rd, Graceville
item.Description: Delivered as the signature artworks for the Graceville Suburban Centre Renewal Project in 2016. The sculpture has three different viewing heads/portals. Inside each head is a simple automata theatrette featuring a local scene in the throes of disaster.

item.Itemtitle: The Human Factor Series - The Trapeze Artist
item.Itemtitlelist: T, The Human Factor Series - The Trapeze Artist
item.Description: The Human Factor series was created by Brisbane's Artbusters in the late 1980s. The first iterations of this iconic series were installed throughout the World Expo 88 site.

item.Itemtitle: The Human Factor Series - The Juggler
item.Itemtitlelist: T, The Human Factor Series - The Juggler
item.Description: The cushion is designed with a tartan pattern that appropriates the surrounding geographical Streets, Avenues and Terraces of the Paddington district. It also honours the history the original inhabitants, the Turrbal people, whose pathways were built upon to form Brisbane's road infrastructure.

item.Location: Market and Charlotte Streets, Brisbane CBD
item.Description: The title 'Rapture' suggests the uplifting potential that is human connectivity, a potential implicit in a modern city. The sculpture depicts two attenuated figures connecting at the heads in a dynamic vertical cantilever.
Flow was originally commissioned for the Creation Symposium held in New Farm Park in 2006, and relocated to Kangaroo Point in 2017.

item.Location: Cnr Fish Lane and Hope Street, South Brisbane
item.Description: Steam Machine celebrates the industrial and architectural heritage of the Fish Lane Precinct. Two abstracted jets of 'steam' make direct reference to the historical Brisbane Steam Laundry and the Eodone Aerated Water Company, which were sited nearby.

item.Itemtitle: The Human Factor Series - The Botanists
item.Itemtitlelist: T, The Human Factor Series - The Botanists
item.Description: The Human Factor series was created by Brisbane's Artbusters in the late 1980s. The first iterations of this iconic series were installed throughout the World Expo 88 site.

Remainder No. 20 and No. 21 sphere's appear eroded, revealing highly textured aggregate beneath their smooth surface. They exist in a state between the natural and the fabricated, a metaphor for the highly altered natural environments that we live in today.
Large Arcs is minimal in form, smooth matte brass sweeping upwards towards the sky. It is in contrast to the organic and unpredictable beauty of the plants both in the work and around it.

item.Itemtitle: The Human Factor Series - The Dancers
item.Itemtitlelist: T, The Human Factor Series - The Dancers
item.Description: The Human Factor series was created by Brisbane's Artbusters in the late 1980s. The first iterations of this iconic series were installed throughout the World Expo 88 site.
Bronze sculpture depicting Dr Clem Jones, donated by the Clem Jones Group in 2018.

item.Itemtitle: The Human Factor Series - The Drover and His Mate
item.Itemtitlelist: T, The Human Factor Series - The Drover and His Mate
item.Location: Cnr King Street & Gregory Terrace, Bowen Hills
item.Description: The first iterations of this iconic series were installed throughout the World Expo '88 site. The figures seen today have been re-imaged and re-cast, giving residents and visitors a sense of the celebration that World Expo '88 was.

item.Material: stainless steel, perspex, resin, pressed aluminium, buttons, glass beads, LEDs, custom code and electronics
item.Description: You Are My Sunshine adorns Beirne Lane in Brisbane's Fortitude Valley like a giant, hand-crafted jewel that glistens with colour-changing light. The artwork materially captures the memories associated with these spaces using pressed metal and decorative glass commonly found in Queensland architecture, together with a centre-piece of buttons and glass beads that once might have been sold by TC Beirne.

item.Itemtitle: The magic lies in the spaces between
item.Itemtitlelist: T, The magic lies in the spaces between
item.Description: The magic lies in the spaces between responds to the vibrant, multicultural community of Mount Gravatt, celebrating difference, embracing the unknown and seeing the spaces between us for the magic they can create

item.Itemtitle: The Human Factor Series - Be Prepared
item.Itemtitlelist: T, The Human Factor Series - Be Prepared
item.Location: Scout Place, River Terrace, Kangaroo Point
item.Description: The first iterations of this iconic series were installed throughout the World Expo '88 site. The figures seen today have been re-imaged and re-cast, giving residents and visitors a sense of the celebration that World Expo '88 was. Donated by Scout's Queensland, 2019.
Gone to Seed is a vibrant, husk-like form that references the complex seed and fruit forms in the Botanic Gardens.

item.Location: Cnr Old Cleveland & Cavendish Roads, Coorparoo
item.Description: The Roxy Theatre once stood here in the exciting early days of Cinema. Pop! celebrates the return of cinema to this corner of Coorparoo. Through the quintessential movie snack, popcorn, the artwork is a playful reference to our sensory connection to cinema.

In Roots, Hiromi Tango explores relationships between people, nature and environments. She worked with the staff and volunteers of the Brisbane Botanic Gardens, Mt Coot-tha to understand the history and stories of place, and to access diver se plant species. A selection of species was cast from life in bronze to create the final sculpture, which offers a reflection of our connectedness and growth to nature and each other.

The Magnificent Flying Machines are a series of 10 colourful art beacons created by Kenji Uranishi – a Japanese-born, Brisbane-based artist. The art beacons were designed to be wayfinding markers along Kingsford Smith Drive to encourage residents and visitors to explore the area. The colourful art beacons take inspiration from two ground-breaking Australian aviators and Hamilton locals – Sir Charles Kingsford Smith and Maude 'Lores' Bonney.

item.Itemtitle: bandarra-gan chidna: strong woman track / track of strong women
item.Itemtitlelist: b, bandarra-gan chidna: strong woman track / track of strong women
item.Description: bandarra-gan chidna: strong woman track is a series of ground plane artworks by Brisbane artist Judy Watson. It highlights the stories and histories of Brisbane women through imagery that reflects their often-overlooked contributions.
Re-wild is a festooning of twelve Convovulus flowers cast in concrete along Norman creek. The flowers reference historical accounts of the species and celebrate revegetation of this natural waterway.

The name Bulimba is from the Yuggera/Goenpul word meaning Place of Magpie-lark, or Peewee. This series of lightboxes depict Peewee birds that work pairs to create their home and remind us of the importance of working together.

Ride with me draws on the history and wildlife of the Camp Hill area. The place was an early resting place, where people camped on the journey between the city and the sea and used the natural world to find our way. The artwork is three groups of animals that guide and connect us, encouraging interaction, conversation and exchange.

Native Still Life is inspired by the stunning native flowers that exist in context of the Botanical Gardens and responds to how historically, these flowers have been visually associated with Aboriginal People through western home décor, adding to the construction of our national identity.

The name of Ashgrove is derived from the Black Ash tree, which is characteristic to the local area and found growing throughout the suburb. These sculptures depict a botanical interpretation of Black Ash tree seed pods in various stages of formation. From a solid pod shape to a splitting pod and finally to an open star fish shape. The artworks create harmony in the landscape by showing this evolution and natural process of change.

Introducing Kooka!, a playful nod to an Australian icon and a Paddington neighbourhood local, leading you on a creative trail along the Paddington terraces, celebrating their unique character and heritage.

item.Location: Cnr Given Tce & Campbell St, Paddington
item.Description: Introducing Kooka!, a playful nod to an Australian icon and a Paddington neighbourhood local, leading you on a creative trail along the Paddington terraces, celebrating their unique character and heritage.

item.Location: Cnr Given Tce & Hayward St, Paddington
item.Description: Introducing Kooka!, a playful nod to an Australian icon and a Paddington neighbourhood local, leading you on a creative trail along the Paddington terraces, celebrating their unique character and heritage.

item.Description: Introducing Kooka!, a playful nod to an Australian icon and a Paddington neighbourhood local, leading you on a creative trail along the Paddington terraces, celebrating their unique character and heritage.

item.Description: Introducing Kooka!, a playful nod to an Australian icon and a Paddington neighbourhood local, leading you on a creative trail along the Paddington terraces, celebrating their unique character and heritage.

item.Itemtitle: Meet the neighbours - wings of Paddington & A bugs life
item.Itemtitlelist: M, Meet the neighbours - wings of Paddington & A bugs life
item.Location: Cnr Latrobe Tce & Alma St, Paddington
item.Description: Introducing Kooka!, a playful nod to an Australian icon and a Paddington neighbourhood local, leading you on a creative trail along the Paddington terraces, celebrating their unique character and heritage.

item.Description: Introducing Kooka!, a playful nod to an Australian icon and a Paddington neighbourhood local, leading you on a creative trail along the Paddington terraces, celebrating their unique character and heritage.

item.Itemtitle: Kooka! In the hood & Confetti Kooka!
item.Itemtitlelist: K, Kooka! In the hood & Confetti Kooka!

item.Description: Introducing Kooka!, a playful nod to an Australian icon and a Paddington neighbourhood local, leading you on a creative trail along the Paddington terraces, celebrating their unique character and heritage.

item.Itemtitlelist: L, Love the view from here
item.Description: Introducing Kooka!, a playful nod to an Australian icon and a Paddington neighbourhood local, leading you on a creative trail along the Paddington terraces, celebrating their unique character and heritage.

item.Itemtitle: Well the plants will be happy
item.Itemtitlelist: W, Well the plants will be happy
item.Description: Introducing Kooka!, a playful nod to an Australian icon and a Paddington neighbourhood local, leading you on a creative trail along the Paddington terraces, celebrating their unique character and heritage.

Introducing Kooka!, a playful nod to an Australian icon and a Paddington neighbourhood local, leading you on a creative trail along the Paddington terraces, celebrating their unique character and heritage.

item.Location: Sir Thomas Brisbane Planetarium, Mt Coot-tha
item.Description: Abigail Allwood is an internationally recognised geologist and astrobiologist. The artwork depicts Abigail Allwood deep in contemplation, equipped with her geological tools, and is in recognition of her distinguished career in science.

item.Location: Corner Baroona Rd and Nash Street, Paddington
item.Description: Rosie-Lee is a vibrant artwork that references the name of Rosalie and its associated iconography, overlaid with references to local historical and geographical sites and the social culture of the area.
A series of five artworks that use official signate templates to jump start dull meanings into new visual poetics as conversation starters.
